answer:
\boxed{A}

Palmitic acid is a long-chain fatty acid that enters gluconeogenesis only after being converted to acetyl-CoA, which cannot be directly used for gluconeogenesis due to the absence of carboxylase enzymes to extend it to oxaloacetate. The other options (pyruvate, galactose, glycerol, propionic acid, lactic acid, aspartic acid, alanine, glutamic acid, and oxaloacetate) can all be converted into intermediates of the gluconeogenic pathway.
